note_data.title: Chemical formulae and equations
keyPoints: A **chemical formula** shows the types and numbers of atoms present in a compound using chemical symbols
A **chemical equation** shows the reactants and products in a chemical reaction with correct formulae and balanced numbers
The **state symbols** (s), (l), (g), and (aq) must be included to show the physical state of each substance
**Balancing equations** requires equal numbers of each type of atom on both sides of the equation
**Ionic compounds** are named by stating the metal ion first, then the non-metal ion with '-ide' or '-ate' ending
**Molecular compounds** use prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-) to indicate the number of atoms of each element
The **law of conservation of mass** states that atoms cannot be created or destroyed in chemical reactions
**Word equations** show the names of reactants and products, while **symbol equations** use chemical formulae
**Coefficients** (large numbers) in front of formulae show the number of molecules or formula units involved

definitions.definition: A representation showing the types and numbers of atoms present in a compound using chemical symbols and subscript numbers
A representation of a chemical reaction showing reactants and products with their correct formulae and balanced coefficients
A formula that shows the actual number of atoms of each element in one molecule of a compound
Letters in brackets that show the physical state: (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous solution
A chemical equation where the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides of the equation

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the chemical formula or equation exactly as required with correct symbols and numbers
Add coefficients to make equal numbers of each atom type on both sides
Give the systematic chemical name following naming rules
Give the information requested clearly in one sentence

modelAnswer.question: Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid to produce magnesium chloride and hydrogen gas. Include state symbols.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct formulae for all reactants and products

commonMistakes: Never write coefficients as subscripts - 2H₂O not H₄O
Students often forget state symbols or use incorrect ones like (d) for dissolved
Writing unbalanced equations and thinking they are complete
Confusing molecular formulae with empirical formulae
Changing subscripts when balancing instead of adding coefficients
examinerTips: Chemical equations appear in every paper - practice balancing daily
Always check your balanced equation by counting atoms on each side
State symbols are often worth separate marks - never omit them
Learn common ion formulae and charges to write correct compound formulae
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: No specific diagram required, but students should be able to represent particle models showing conservation of atoms in chemical reactions
